original_abstract:
Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) represent the leading non-viral platform for gene delivery, yet the specific influence of their physicochemical properties on performance following intramuscular (IM) injection remains poorly understood. In this study, we engineered a series of LNPs with varying sizes, surface charges, and PEGylated lipid compositions to evaluate their transfection efficiency and biodistribution using luciferase mRNA (mLuc) as a reporter.

Our findings demonstrate that smaller LNPs and those with a neutral surface charge achieve significantly higher transfection levels after IM administration. Interestingly, while these factors dictated protein expression, they did not substantially alter the overall anatomical distribution of the particles themselves. We further observed that PEGylated lipids with shorter acyl chains (C14 vs. C18) markedly enhanced transfection by facilitating more efficient cellular uptake and endosomal escape. Detailed mechanistic analysis revealed that the choice of PEGylated lipid dictates the specific endocytic pathway—clathrin-mediated vs. caveolae-mediated—primarily due to differences in the rate of PEG shedding.

By systematic evaluation of these parameters, this work identifies the critical role of nanoparticle architecture and PEG lipid kinetics in optimizing the intramuscular route. These insights resolve key knowledge gaps and provide specific design principles for maximizing the efficacy of mRNA-based vaccines and therapeutics.
humanized_abstract:
Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) remain the predominate non-viral delivery platform for gene therapy, and understanding the effect of the formulation properties post intramuscular (IM) injection is limited. We prepared and characterized a panel of LNP with controlled diameter, charge, and PEGylated lipid content in order to assess mRNA translation and trafficking in vivo following IM delivery of luciferase mRNA, mLuc.

Here we show that small LNPs with neutral surface charge yielded the highest transgene expression following in vivo IM injection. Despite similar transgene expression, the anatomical distribution of LNP did not differ between the various formulations tested. We also identified that PEG-lipids substituted with shorter acyl chains (C14) versus longer acyl chains (C18) improved gene expression through increased uptake and endosomal release. Detailed studies on the endocytic pathways of these formulated LNP revealed that the choice of PEG-lipid is primarily responsible for directing LNP uptake into clathrin or caveolae mediated endocytosis pathways, which was dictated by the rate of PEG shedding.

Here, by analyzing all the parameters, we show that the architecture of nanoparticles and the kinetics of PEG lipids are essential for the intramuscular route. Our study addresses important knowledge gaps in the field and therefore provides explicit design criteria for achieving optimal performance of mRNA-based vaccines and therapeutics.
